team_information.team_or_company_name: Chesapeake Bay Cancer Survivors Dragon Boat Team

paddler_information.name: Melissa Anne Carter
paddler_information.medical_condition_description:
I am a 41-year-old breast cancer survivor, currently in remission. I completed chemotherapy in late 2022 and radiation in early 2023. I have regular follow-up appointments every 6 months. My oncologist has cleared me for moderate to vigorous physical activity, including dragon boat paddling, as long as I listen to my body and stay hydrated.

The main ongoing medical issues that might be relevant for event organizers are:

1. Lymphedema risk in left arm: I had lymph nodes removed on my left side, which puts me at higher risk for lymphedema in that arm. I wear a compression sleeve when doing repetitive upper-body activity. If you notice any significant swelling, redness, or if I mention a heavy or tight feeling in my left arm, I may need to take a break, elevate the arm, and possibly remove myself from paddling for the remainder of the day.

2. Mild exercise-induced asthma: I was diagnosed with mild exercise-induced asthma in my late teens. It doesn’t affect me much day-to-day, but intense exertion in hot, humid weather can sometimes trigger shortness of breath or chest tightness. I carry a rescue inhaler (albuterol) in my small dry bag and in my personal gear on shore. If I seem unusually short of breath, wheezy, or if I say I need my inhaler, please allow me a minute or two to use it and rest. I have never been hospitalized for asthma and have never had a severe attack during sports, but I like organizers to be aware.

3. Medication and allergies: I currently take a daily low-dose beta blocker (metoprolol) for slightly elevated blood pressure that developed during treatment, as well as vitamin D and a multivitamin. I do NOT have any known drug or food allergies, and I do not have a history of anaphylaxis.

4. Heat sensitivity and hydration: Since chemo, I notice I am more sensitive to heat and can get fatigued more quickly in direct sun. I am very careful about staying hydrated and wearing a hat and sunscreen, but if I start feeling lightheaded, nauseated, or extremely flushed, I may need to sit in the shade, apply a cool cloth, and sip water or an electrolyte drink.

Overall, I am active—I walk or jog 3–4 times a week and do light strength training—so I do not expect these issues to interfere with normal participation. I am listing them in detail so that if something does come up during practice or race day, the coaches and safety volunteers have context and know that I’m not being dramatic; these are just lingering side effects from treatment that I manage with a bit of extra caution.
